coredata.dc:title: Chlorine triggered de-alloying of AuAg@Carbon nanodots: Towards fabrication of a dual signalling assay combining the plasmonic property of bimetallic alloy nanoparticles and photoluminescence of carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadpour, Zahra
Safavi, Afsaneh
Abdollahi, Seyyed Hossein
coredata.dc:description: Integration of Au-Ag alloy and fluorescent carbon nanodots (C-dots) into a single platform resulted in a new dual sensing assay for chlorine. Selective etching of Ag from AuAg@C-dots was transformed into: (i) colorimetric signal by surface plasmon resonance (SPR) tuning of the alloy and (ii) fluorimetric signal by perturbation of fluorescence energy transfer between C-dots and alloy nanoparticles. Fast oxidizing of silver atoms incorporated in the bimetallic structure induced by chlorine resulted in selective de-alloying of bimetallic hybrid nanoparticles and an intense visible change of the colloidal dispersion color. On the other hand, the systematic change in Au/Ag ratio strongly affected the emission intensity of C-dots in the hybrid structure leading to an enhancement in the fluorescence signal. Thus, the assay enables the detection of chlorine both under visible and UV lights with high sensitivity. The detection limit (DL) values were calculated as 6.2 × 10−7 M and 5.1 × 10−7 M through colorimetric and fluorimetric pathways, respectively. Most importantly, it was demonstrated to be selective over common cations, anions and some reactive oxygen species (ROS). This assay was successfully applied to the determination of chlorine concentration in bleach solution and tap water. It is robust and is suitable for cost effective chlorine measurement in environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic alloy nanoparticles
Carbon nanodots
Hypochlorite
Chlorine
De-alloying
Reactive oxygen species
Visual sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric assay of chlorine using Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots
Fluorescent assay of chlorine using Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots
Application for analysis of ClO− in bleach and tap water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots (A) before and (B) after chlorine addition. (The concentration of chlorine for TEM analysis was 40 μM, pH 8, [NaNO3 −] = 70 mM, [NaHCO3] = 2 mM, 2 h oxygen saturation; inset of panel (A) is the size histogram of hybrid nanoparticles deduced from TEM). Right panels are DLS size analysis of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots before (up) and after (down) addition of chlorine.
Powder XRD of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots before (curve a) and after (curve b) chlorine addition.
UV–Vis absorption decay and SPR shift of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots following the increasing amount of chlorine over the concentration range of 0–60 μM.
Comparison of the assay sensitivity to chlorine using Au@C-dots, Ag@C-dots and Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots.

Digital photographs of nanoparticle dispersions with increasing chlorine concentration from left to right shows the comparison of the assay sensitivity to chlorine using Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots (first row), Ag@C-dots (second row), and Au@C-dots (third row).

(A) Spectral overlap of C-dots emission and Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots absorption spectrum, both peaks are normalized. (B) PL quenching of C-dots after hybridization with bimetallic alloy and signal recovery using excess chlorine (70 μM). (C) Enhancement of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots fluorescence in exposure to various chlorine concentrations (Inset: fluorescence calibration plot). ([NaNO3] = 70 mM, [NaHCO3] = 2 mM, pH 8, and the electrolyte solution was purged by oxygen for 2 h).

(A) The colorimetric and (B) fluorimetric responses of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots to interfering species in the absence of (Blue lines) and coexistence with (Red lines) chlorine. (anions concentration were 2.5 mM, Cations except for Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+ and Cd2+ were 100 μM, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+ and Cd2+ were 50 μM, chlorine was 30 μM and ROS species were 40 μM ([NaNO3] = 70 mM, [NaHCO3] = 2 mM, pH 10, and the electrolyte solution was purged by oxygen for 2 h). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Chlorine-mediated de-alloying of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots. (a) Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots, (b) Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots+ClO−.
Comparison of nanoparticles-based optical probes for the assay of chlorine.
Determination of chlorine in tap water and bleach samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Chlorine concentration measured spectrophotometrically at 290 nm.

head.ce:title: Chlorine triggered de-alloying of AuAg@Carbon nanodots: Towards fabrication of a dual signalling assay combining the plasmonic property of bimetallic alloy nanoparticles and photoluminescence of carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz, 7194684795, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integration of Au-Ag alloy and fluorescent carbon nanodots (C-dots) into a single platform resulted in a new dual sensing assay for chlorine. Selective etching of Ag from AuAg@C-dots was transformed into: (i) colorimetric signal by surface plasmon resonance (SPR) tuning of the alloy and (ii) fluorimetric signal by perturbation of fluorescence energy transfer between C-dots and alloy nanoparticles. Fast oxidizing of silver atoms incorporated in the bimetallic structure induced by chlorine resulted in selective de-alloying of bimetallic hybrid nanoparticles and an intense visible change of the colloidal dispersion color. On the other hand, the systematic change in Au/Ag ratio strongly affected the emission intensity of C-dots in the hybrid structure leading to an enhancement in the fluorescence signal. Thus, the assay enables the detection of chlorine both under visible and UV lights with high sensitivity. The detection limit (DL) values were calculated as 6.2 × 10−7 M and 5.1 × 10−7 M through colorimetric and fluorimetric pathways, respectively. Most importantly, it was demonstrated to be selective over common cations, anions and some reactive oxygen species (ROS). This assay was successfully applied to the determination of chlorine concentration in bleach solution and tap water. It is robust and is suitable for cost effective chlorine measurement in environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of Ag nanoparticles combined with high chemical stability of Au nanoparticles make bimetallic Au-Ag nanoparticles to be both SPR sensitive and chemically stable in harsh conditions [1]. Nanoscale bimetallic Au-Ag with distinctive optical, electrical and chemical properties offers superior advantages in catalysis, antimicrobial properties, and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) sensing compared to their monometallic counterparts [2–4]. Synthesis and optical characteristics of bimetallic Au-Ag nanoparticles have been studied extensively in literature [5–7]. They have been prepared by different strategies mostly by co-reduction of metal salts using various reducing agents [3,8–10]. Recently, Essner et al. reported a green approach for preparation of bimetallic alloyed Au-Ag using carbon nanodots (C-dots) both as reducing and stabilizing agents [11]. In another report, Garg et al. investigated the kinetic of oxidative dissolution of silver nanoparticles by chlorine [12]. It was proved that the kinetic of Ag nanoparticles oxidation in the presence of chlorine is much faster than the rate in the presence of hydrogen peroxide or dioxygen [12].

Inspired by these two reports, we synthesized C-dots stabilized Au-Ag alloy with strong and tunable absorption bands across the visible range of the electromagnetic spectrum. The fast response of Ag nanoparticles to chlorine motivated us to use chlorine for selective removal of Ag from AuxAg1−x@C-dots and design a dual readout sensing system for chlorine. Chlorine is an extensively used disinfectant for water treatment. However, accumulation of this ion in environmental samples is associated with human diseases including neuron degeneration, cancer, arthritis and inflammatory diseases [13–16]. Therefore, developing a fast, selective, sensitive, low cost, visual and portable sensor for measurement of this ion is crucial. To date, several nanoparticle-based probes have been designed for chlorine sensing including carbon nanodots [17], graphene dots [18], quantum dots [19], gold nanoclusters [20], copper nanoclusters [21], gold nanoparticles [22,23] and silver nanoparticles [24,25]. All of these probes are single channel, either colorimetric or fluorimetric. In fluorimetric probes reported so far, the sensing mode is “turn-off” (fluorescence quenching). In this paper, for the first time, we have introduced a dual signalling strategy for both colorimetric and fluorimetric detection of chlorine with widened practical applicability with respect to single channel probes. The turn-on mode (fluorescence enhancement) in the present system takes the advantage of being more sensitive compared with turn-off sensors particularly for use in biological media and cell imaging due to background interference reduction. Moreover, unlike the toxic quantum dots used for chlorine sensing, C-dots used in the present nanohybrid are environmentally friendly. Also, in contrary to gold nanoparticles-based systems, which are subject to aggregation in solutions with high electrolyte concentrations, this nanohybrid is quite stable in strongly saline solutions. Chlorine induced de-alloying of this hybrid bimetallic nanoparticle can be visualized both under visible and UV lights. This assay operates based on a “mix and detect” strategy, is fast, selective, sensitive, simple and robust for environmental monitoring of sodium hypochlorite in bleach and tap water samples.

The overall de-alloying of bimetallic AuxAg1−x@C-dots mediated by chlorine is depicted in Scheme 1 . Selective oxidation of Ag from AuxAg1−x@C-dots was transduced into colorimetric and fluorimetric responses. De-alloying of AuxAg1−x@C-dots nanoparticles was achieved using the fast oxidizing capability of chlorine on silver atoms incorporated in the bimetallic structure. The process made an intense visible change of the colloidal dispersion color from yellow to orange, pink, dark pink, gray and finally to colorless. On the other hand, the change in fluorescence signal of C-dots was used as the second signalling read-out. C-dots by virtue of their great features including water solubility, electron donor/acceptor capability and high photoluminescence (PL) emission intensity have been widely used in different research fields since their first accidental discovery in 2004 [26,27]. C-dots have been hybridized with metallic gold or silver nanoparticles as donor-acceptor energy transfer pairs [28,29]. Also, metal nanoparticles serve as efficient energy acceptors, particularly through the nanometal surface energy transfer (NSET) pathway [30,31]. In this study, fluorescent C-dots are donors while alloyed bimetallic Au-Ag nanoparticles are acceptors. Interestingly, the fine modification of PL signal of C-dots was achieved through the compositional change of alloyed structure by chlorine. Selective removal of Ag from the bimetallic alloy resulted in the red shift of the absorption band of the acceptor. Consequently, signal overlapping between donor-acceptor pairs was reduced, the energy transfer was disrupted and the fluorescence signal was turned on. Based on the fast etching process mediated by chlorine, we demonstrated the use of AuxAg1−x@C-dots as a new dual signalling platform for quantitative measurement of free chlorine in bleach and tap water samples.

Sodium hypochlorite (6–14%), anhydrous citric acid, silver nitrate, sodium hydroxide, sodium bicarbonate, hydrogen peroxide 30%, tert-butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP), sodium peroxide, sodium nitrite, and sodium nitrate were purchased from Merck. Peroxynitrite (ONOO−) was synthesized freshly following a literature procedure [32,33]. Briefly, solutions of (i) 0.6 M NaNO2 and (ii) 0.6 M HCl/0.7 M H2O2 were simultaneously pumped into a T-junction and mixed. Peroxynitrous acid was produced through the acid-catalyzed reaction of nitrous acid and H2O2. Immediately after mixing, the reaction mixture was poured into 3 M of NaOH that was kept in an ice bath and quenched. The yellow reaction product was frozen at −20 °C and used within 1 week. The concentration of peroxynitrite was determined spectrophotometrically (ε 302 nm = 1670 M−1 cm−1) [32].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured using a Shimadzu spectrophotometer and a 1-cm path length quartz cuvette. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on a Hitachi S4160 device. FTIR spectrum was recorded using a Shimadzu FTIR 8000 series. The composition of bimetallic alloy was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a X'Pert PRO MPD diffractometer (Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54060 Å), step size of 0.02° and 20 s step time for each point). Size distribution measurements were carried out on a Zetasizer Nano ZS from Malvern Company. E-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was performed using a VEGA∖∖TESCAN-XMU device. A Perkin Elmer PL spectrophotometer (LS45) with a 1-cm quartz cell was used for measuring the PL spectra. Dialysis tubing (D0530) was received from Sigma.

A chemical reduction process proceeded for production of AuxAg1−x@C-dots [11]. 7 mL of 0.43 mg mL−1 of aqueous C-dots solution was heated to boiling under reflux condition for 15 min. AgNO3 and Au(OH)4 − were used as the metal salts precursors. Different Au:Ag ratios were made by simultaneously addition of aliquots of 10 mM of both metal salts. Au(OH)4 − was prepared by the addition of X mL of 10 mM of HAuCl4 to 2 mL of 100 mM NaOH. (1-X) mL of 10 mM of AgNO3 and the resulting Au(OH)4 − solution were simultaneously added to the hot C-dot solution. For example, Au:Ag (40:60) was prepared by mixing 0.4 mL of 10 mM HAuCl4 with 2 mL of 100 mM NaOH to produce 2.4 mL of Au(OH)4 −. 0.6 mL of AgNO3 and 2.4 mL of Au(OH)4 − were simultaneously added to 7 mL of C-dot solution. So, the final concentration of C-dots was 0.3 mg mL−1. After the metal salts addition to the boiling C-dots, stirring and heating was continued for an additional 15 min. The color of the reaction mixture was gradually changed from yellow to brown. After 15 min, the mixture was cooled down to room temperature. To remove ionic species and also for adjusting the pH of nanohybrid to about 6–7, the mixture was dialyzed against deionized water overnight. Other Au:Ag ratios of 80: 20, 60: 40, 50: 50, and 20: 80 were prepared similarly by varying X. The procedures for the synthesized monometallic Au@C-dots and Ag@C-dots has been explained in detail in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric assay of chlorine using Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: In all experiments, the buffer solution was saturated with molecular oxygen. The buffer solution was composed of sodium bicarbonate and sodium nitrate. The final pH value was adjusted to 8. For measurement of ClO−, the as-synthesized bimetallic nanoparticle solution was added dropwise to the electrolyte (70 mM sodium nitrate, 2 mM sodium carbonate) with the volume ratio of 5.7:1 (electrolyte: hybrid). The yellow-brownish solution was then purged with oxygen for 2 h. 1 mL of O2-saturated colloidal nanoparticle solution was added to the glass vials followed by the addition of ClO− standard solution with different concentrations. After 10 min incubation at room temperature, the absorption spectra were collected. The standard solution of NaOCl was prepared freshly before use and the concentration was determined after dilution of stock NaOCl (6–14%, Merck) and measuring the UV absorbance value at 290 nm (ε 290 = 350 M−1 cm−1) [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent assay of chlorine using Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: 450 μL of the bimetallic nanoparticles solution was diluted to 3 mL by the electrolyte (70 mM sodium nitrate, 2 mM sodium carbonate, pH 8). The resulting solution was transferred to a quartz cuvette and oxygenated for 1 h. 5 μL of ClO− standard solution was added and the PL spectrum was measured after 10 min incubation at room temperature. The excitation wavelength was 340 nm and the emission intensity was measured at 440 nm. For each data point, incremental addition of ClO− standard solution to the cuvette was continued and the solution PL was measured after 10 min.

C-dots were synthesized via a protocol reported by Essner et al. [11]. Anhydrous citric acid as the carbon precursor was thermally decomposed and the final product was obtained after purification and a final step of freeze drying. The TEM image of C-dots shows semi-spherical morphology. Size distribution histogram shows the average particle diameter of 5.2 nm (45 particles) (Fig. S-1A, S-1B). The n-π* transition of CO bonds accounts for the C-dots small shoulder appeared at around 300 nm and the increasing optical absorption in the UV range is attributed to π-π* transitions (Fig. S-1C) [35,36]. Excitation-dependent emission of the synthesized C-dots in this work is consistent with typically observed PL properties of C-dots [37,38]. The maximum emission intensity of citrate-derived C-dots was observed at 440 nm with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. FTIR spectrum of citrate-derived C-dots indicates the presence of oxygenated functional groups (carboxyl, ether, ester, epoxy, and alcohol) on the surface of C-dots (Fig. S-1D) which give them good water solubility. The XRD pattern of C-dots shows a broad small peak at 2θ = 18° as a clear signature of amorphous carbon structure (Fig. S-2) [39].

The co-reduction of varying mole fractions of HAuCl4 and AgNO3 using citrate derived C-dots as effective reducing agents resulted in bimetallic hybrid nanoparticles with tailored SPR bands. TEM image and the particle size histogram of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots (Fig. 1 A) showed that the average particle size was 5.6 nm (150 particles) which was in close agreement with the average size measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (6–7 nm). Large aggregated particles after treatment with chlorine were observed in the sample (Fig. 1B) in support of the particle agglomeration confirmed by DLS. Dissolution of silver nanoparticles by chlorine has been investigated [12,40,41]. The agglomerated particles with irregular morphologies looked to be adhered with no obvious boundary between particles. Particles looked to be fused into each other so the surface looked smooth with no interparticle distinct separation. This bridge formation has been observed previously and attributed to deposition of AgCl layer on the aggregated Ag nanoparticles [42,43]. The existence of AgCl was further confirmed by EDX and XRD analysis.

The analysis result of EDX revealed that both Au and Ag coexist in the composition of C-dots stabilized bimetallic alloy. According to the EDX of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots, the actual atomic ratio of Ag/Au was 58.7/41.3 which was in close agreement with the molar ratio of [AgNO3]/[HAuCl4] (60/40) in the initial feed solution. After treatment with chlorine, the presence of Cl was confirmed by EDX (Fig. S-3). The elemental fractions of Au, Ag, and Cl were measured to be 44.22, 42.17, and 13.61%, respectively. Since the initial nanoparticles concentrations for both samples (before and after chlorine addition) were identical, the difference in peak intensity of the EDX pattern in two cases can be implemented to exploit the reaction mechanism. The elemental ratio of Ag/Au was decreased from 1.42 to 0.95. This observation implies the selective dissolution of Ag from the bimetallic alloy nanoparticles.

The crystalline nature of bimetallic alloy was investigated by XRD analysis. Similar lattice constants of Au and Ag (4.078 Å for Au, 4.086 Å for Ag) and complete miscibility of these metals make their lattice planes to be so close and hard to distinguish [7,44,45]. The XRD pattern of bimetallic alloy (Fig. 2 , curve a) shows four characteristic peaks at 38.2°, 44.4°, 64.5°, and 77.5° indexed as the (111), (200), (220), and (311) lattice planes originated from the face centered cubic (fcc) structure [6,46,47]. The change in crystal structure of nanoparticles upon chlorine addition was also studied (Fig. 2, curve b). Along with the lattice planes of bimetallic alloy, the chlorine treated sample showed extra lattice planes of AgCl marked by the 2Ɵ values of 27.8°, 32.2° and 46.2° confirmed the formation of AgCl upon reaction with chlorine.

Under the optimized experimental conditions (please see the Supporting Information (Table S-1, Fig. S-4, S-5)), the spectral change of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots upon exposure to different concentrations of chlorine followed a linear trend over the chlorine concentration range of 1.7–19 μM (Absorbance = −19.23 × 10−3 [ClO− (μM)] + 0.7241; R2 = 0.9980). The SPR red shift of the hybrid and a decrease in absorption peak intensity clarified the decrease in Ag amount of bimetallic alloy (Fig. 3 ). In fact, gradual dissolution of Ag altered the level of alloying and synergistic plasmonic cooperation of two metals. The theoretical detection limit (DL) calculated by the formula of 3σ/m was 6.2 × 10−7 M and the RSD for 3 replicate measurements was 2.5%.

To evaluate the impact of alloying on the extent of visual color change, we measured the chlorine-induced spectral change of both Au@C-dots and Ag@C-dots with the same amount of metal precursors used in the bimetallic nanohybrids (0.4 mM for Au and 0.6 mM for Ag) (Fig. 4 ).

Interestingly, no noticeable spectral change was observed when Au@C-dots were exposed to chlorine with concentrations up to 30 μM. However, at higher chlorine concentrations, partial color degradation of Au@C-dots took place presumably due to the enhanced oxidation power of chlorine at high concentrations. Unlike Au@C-dots, the absorption intensity of Ag@C-dots decreased upon increasing chlorine while the peak position was almost unchanged, which is ascribed to etching of Ag and formation of AgCl (Fig. S-6). However, at low chlorine concentrations up to 12 μM, the absorption intensity did not appreciably drop compared with bimetallic alloy nanoparticles. This difference is due to the fact that chlorine (EºHOCl/Cl − 1.49 V) [41] is a better oxidant for Ag (EºAg + /Ag(s) 0.7994 V) rather than Au (EºAu 3+ /Au(s) 1.5 V). Au@C-dots remained mostly pink even at high chlorine concentrations and the color change of Ag@C-dots did not seem to be sharp. However, the bimetallic alloy showed an intense color change spanned from yellow to orange, pink, dark pink, gray and nearly colorless (Fig. 5 ).

These control experiments using monometallic nanoparticles revealed that dissolution of Ag atoms incorporated into the bimetallic structure accounts for the sharp spectral shift of the hybrid when treated with chlorine. In fact, the stoichiometric ratio of Au/Ag and subsequently the maximum absorption wavelength was tailored by chlorine. As the Ag atoms removed and the Au amounts in the bimetallic structure enhanced, the SPR peak red shifted and broadened and the peak intensity decreased. It is interesting to note that the sensitivity of chlorine detection using bimetallic nanoparticles was better than the monometallic nanoparticles. The calculated DL of chlorine using monometallic Ag@C-dots was 1.63 × 10−6 M, while the DL for bimetallic Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots was 6.2 × 10−7 M. The reinforced plasmon response, particularly at low chlorine concentrations, is due to synergy between Au and Ag which enables enhanced optical response of the hybrid alloy.

As presented in Scheme 1, Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots exhibited a green fluorescence while chlorine treated sample shows a blue color under UV irradiation. The luminescence color change of C-dots (from blue to green and vice versa) through reduction-oxidation cycle has been observed. Also, tuning the luminescence of C-dots from blue to green light has been reported using H2O2 as oxidizing agent [43,52]. While oxidation results in red shift in the emission wavelength of C-dots and the color change from blue to green, reduction operates in the reverse direction. C-dots used in the present study were synthesized using citric acid as the precursor. The reducing property of citrated derived C-dots was verified by simultaneous reduction of metal salts into bimetallic alloy. It could be proposed that when the blue luminescent C-dots are oxidized by metal salts, their emission peak red shifts and the color under UV irradiation changes to green. Conversely, after partial removal of metallic Ag, the luminescence color returns back to blue.

For the interference study, we tested common coexisting anions and cations in water samples as well as reactive oxygen species (ROS). They were added to the colloidal nanoparticles dispersion in the absence and presence of chlorine under the same experimental conditions (Fig. 7 A). Results revealed that none of these interfering species affect the color of bimetallic nanoparticles significantly except for chlorine which rapidly changed the solution color from yellow to dark pink. Anions including S2O8 2−, HPO4 2−, Cl−, BrO−, ClO4 −, ClO3 − and HCOO− with high concentrations of 2.5 mM induced no color response. Also, cations including heavy metal cations induced no spectral change. Most notably, no reactivity of bimetallic alloy towards some ROS species including TBHP, O2 2−, H2O2, ONOO− was observed. Similarly, the fluorimetric responses towards interfering species (Fig. 7B) confirm the high selectivity of this assay and its applicability for chlorine sensing in environmental water samples. It is noteworthy that the system response to chlorine over the alkaline pH range of 8–11 is not affected by pH. So by working in the upper extreme pH values, it is feasible to eliminate the possible interferences of cations via their precipitation as hydroxides.

According to the results presented so far, it could be deduced that selective etching of Ag atoms from Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots structure results in increasing Au/Ag mole ratio and a systematic SPR red shift (450 nm–487 nm). However, the spectral red shift stops as 487 nm, far from the SPR band of pure Au@C-dots at 537 nm. This indicates that the complete removal of Ag atoms do not take place in our case. A similar behavior has been observed and a model was proposed for de-alloying of Au-Ag bimetallic nanoparticles [44]. According to this model, de-alloying of Au-Ag by removal of Ag atoms results in rearrangement of lattice structure. So, Au atoms mostly rely on the outer shell and Ag atoms surrounded by Au neighbour atoms. By increasing the Au/Ag mole ratio, the inner Ag atoms are more protected by Au outer shells. Therefore, further oxidation of inner Ag atoms stops at some point before the complete Ag removal by the oxidant.

The mechanism and kinetics of oxidative reaction of Ag nanoparticles in exposure to hypochlorite ion has been well established in literature [12,40,41,53]. The implications of this reaction can be dissolution of Ag nanoparticles to produce Ag (I), aggregation or precipitation in the form of AgCl. Sodium hypochlorite has been proven to oxidize Ag nanoparticles faster than hydrogen peroxide and oxygen linked to its higher standard electrode potential (Eº) value [41,54]. Exposure to hypochlorite chemically transforms Ag nanoparticles into AgCl [40,41]. Similarly for Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots we observed aggregation and AgCl formation evidenced by TEM, EDX, and XRD analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for analysis of ClO− in bleach and tap water

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, integrated multifunctional property of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots was implemented for selective dual sensing of chlorine. The compositional variation of the hybrid alloy nanostructure was transformed into a visible color change accompanied by the luminescence enhancement. An interesting feature of the hybrid alloy was signal amplification with respect to monometallic nanoparticles owing to the cooperative effect between metallic Au and Ag. SPR sensitivity and chemical stability of Au0.4Ag0.6@C-dots in high electrolyte concentration combined with its high selectivity make this assay to be useful for chlorine analysis in water samples. The reaction mechanism proposed to proceed through selective dissolution of silver from the bimetallic structure and its chemical transformation into AgCl.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude to Iran's National Elites Foundation and Shiraz University Research Council for the support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Details on experimental section, characterization data, optimization experiments, Figs and extended discussion as noted in text.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.12.036.
